critic_raw:
This CNN vs. traditional features cancer cell detection design has a significant data leakage problem and an evaluation flaw.

Normalization statistics — pixel mean and std — are computed from the entire dataset before splitting and applied to all sets. The same issue applies to handcrafted feature standardization: statistics are fit on the entire dataset. This is explicit in the design and is justified by 'stability and consistency.' For an image classification task, pixel normalization leakage is small but nonzero. For the handcrafted features (color histograms, Gabor responses, LBP histograms, Haralick features), the leakage is more consequential: these features have richer distributional structure that varies by tissue type, staining protocol, and scanner. Fitting standardization on the full dataset including test images allows test-set feature statistics to influence the normalization applied during training, reducing the independence of the test evaluation.

The model selection and final training protocol introduces a compound problem. The design specifies that after hyperparameter selection using the validation set, both models are retrained on the combined training + validation set (9,500 images). The CNN uses early stopping based on validation sensitivity as the stopping criterion during the hyperparameter search phase. When the CNN is retrained on train+validation combined, there is no longer a held-out set to determine early stopping — the protocol does not specify how many epochs to train for in this final retraining step. Common choices (using the same epoch count as the best validation run, or training until some fixed schedule) have different properties and can significantly affect test performance. For logistic regression, retraining on train+validation with the selected C is straightforward, but for the CNN this is a meaningful underspecified step.

The primary metric is sensitivity at an unspecified operating threshold. The design says 'a threshold is chosen on the validation set to achieve 0.95 specificity' but sensitivity is then reported on the test set using that same threshold. The flaw is that the threshold is tuned on the validation set distribution, and if the test set has different class prevalence or feature distribution (which is likely given multi-hospital data), the 0.95 specificity constraint will not hold on the test set — yet the design only reports sensitivity, not whether the specificity constraint was satisfied on the test set.

The stratified random split by (hospital site, class label) does not prevent patients from appearing in both training and test sets if the same patient contributed multiple images over the study period. Medical imaging datasets frequently have multiple slides or images per patient. If inter-patient splits are not enforced, the training set may contain images from the same patients in the test set, inflating performance through patient-level memorization.
all_issues_raised: Normalization and handcrafted feature standardization statistics are fit on the full dataset before splitting, constituting preprocessing leakage — the test set's feature distribution informs the normalization applied to training data.

The CNN is retrained on train+validation combined for final evaluation, but the early stopping criterion is undefined for this final training run — this is an underspecified step that can materially affect test performance depending on epoch count choice.

The specificity constraint (≥0.95) is established on the validation set threshold but not verified to hold on the test set — if test set class prevalence or feature distribution differs, the constraint may not hold, making the sensitivity comparison invalid.

The stratified random split by (hospital site, class label) does not enforce patient-level exclusivity — if patients contributed multiple images, the same patient may appear in both training and test sets, enabling patient-level memorization.
all_issues_adjudicated: Normalization and handcrafted feature standardization statistics are fit on the full dataset before splitting, constituting preprocessing leakage — the test set's feature distribution informs the normalization applied to training data.

The CNN is retrained on train+validation combined for final evaluation, but the early stopping criterion is undefined for this final training run — this is an underspecified step that can materially affect test performance depending on epoch count choice.

The specificity constraint (≥0.95) is established on the validation set threshold but not verified to hold on the test set — if test set class prevalence or feature distribution differs, the constraint may not hold, making the sensitivity comparison invalid.

The stratified random split by (hospital site, class label) does not enforce patient-level exclusivity — if patients contributed multiple images, the same patient may appear in both training and test sets, enabling patient-level memorization.
